title: Is the mehndi (henna) ceremony before weddings permissible despite its Hindu origins?
stanceSummary: Mehndi is permissible despite Hindu origins because the religious connotation has been completely lost. It is now purely cultural with no association to Hinduism among Muslims.
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2025 video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LcYRbKaXzKU) (starting at [37:07](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LcYRbKaXzKU&t=2227s)), Yasir Qadhi used the mehndi ceremony as a key example of how practices with pagan origins become permissible when the religious association is lost. His position is:

- **Mehndi is permissible** despite having Hindu origins
- The practice is **100% straight from Hinduism** — in Hindu Indian culture there is a religious festival centered around it
- However, **the religious connotation has been completely lost** among Muslims of India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh
- When Muslims do mehndi, they have **absolutely no indication or association with Hinduism** — it is purely cultural
- This is a clear example of the principle that **what matters is the current practice, not the historical origin**
- As long as what takes place at the mehndi is halal (no impermissible mixing, alcohol, etc.), the event itself is halal
- There is practically **unanimous consensus** among South Asian Muslim scholars and communities that mehndi is permissible

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Hindu Origins of Mehndi — [37:07](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LcYRbKaXzKU&t=2227s)

Sisters, where does your mehndi come from? What is the origin of the mehndi? Who can tell me? Yes — exactly. **Hinduism. Through and through. 100% straight from Hinduism.** In fact, the whole yellow coloring and dyeing — it's straight from Hinduism. The whole rituals and culture — that's why in Hindu Indian culture to this day there is a religious festival that is essentially the mehndi.

And the Muslims of India, when they embraced Islam — so the Pakistani, Bengali, Indian Muslims — all of them have the mehndi ceremony to this day. Well, it's not a ritual, it's a celebration.

### The Ruling on Mehndi — [38:05](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LcYRbKaXzKU&t=2285s)

So is it halal or haram, sisters? See, there's unanimous consensus that it's halal. Now somebody's going to come and say, "Oh, but there's haram that takes place." What did we just say? Tertiary issues — don't bring them in. If you're going to drink alcohol, don't bring in mehndi. It's haram anyway. If there's going to be impermissible gender mixing, don't bring it in. It's haram anyway.

So you can do a mehndi without dancing between the genders, without haram issues like this. **If we want to have a mehndi event before the wedding — every single religious family pretty much does it.** Yeah, there are some that say haram, but by and large they do that.

### Why It's Halal Despite Hindu Origins — [38:51](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LcYRbKaXzKU&t=2331s)

Even though the origin is palpably, recognizably Hindu — **it's halal. Because why?** Because the Muslims when they do it have absolutely no indication of any religious association. **The cultural connotations have gone for it to be religious.** Who cares what the origins were? It's now practiced in a way that is devoid of religion.
